name: Susanoo
description: Susanoo-no-Mikoto, often simply called Susanoo, is a complex and powerful deity in Japanese mythology. Born from Izanagi's nose after his descent to Yomi, the land of the dead, Susanoo is the brother of Amaterasu, the sun goddess, and Tsukuyomi, the moon god. He is primarily known as the god of storms and the sea, but his character is multifaceted, encompassing both destructive and heroic aspects. His tumultuous relationship with his siblings, particularly Amaterasu, is central to many myths. Susanoo's rebellious nature and initial disregard for societal harmony led to his exile from the heavens. However, his subsequent acts of bravery, such as slaying the monstrous Yamata no Orochi, redeemed him and solidified his place as a vital figure in Japanese folklore. He is revered for his strength, his skill with the sword, and his ultimate contribution to humanity by saving a princess and founding a new lineage. Susanoo's story is one of transformation, highlighting the potential for even the most unruly forces to be harnessed for good. He represents the raw power of nature and the importance of balance within the cosmos, a reminder that destruction can pave the way for creation.
subtitle: Storm God of Japanese Mythology

keyMyths.title: Birth of Susanoo
keyMyths.description: Susanoo was born from Izanagi's nose after he returned from the underworld. His birth caused the land to tremble and the gods to fear him.

keyMyths.title: The Contest with Amaterasu
keyMyths.description: Susanoo's disruptive behavior led to a contest with his sister, Amaterasu. His trickery caused Amaterasu to hide in a cave, plunging the world into darkness.

keyMyths.title: Slaying of Yamata no Orochi
keyMyths.description: Susanoo slayed the eight-headed serpent, Yamata no Orochi, saving a princess and obtaining the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi sword. This act redeemed him and established him as a hero.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: An English translation of the Kojiki, Japan's oldest chronicle.
sources.text: An English translation of the Kojiki, Japan's oldest chronicle.

sources.author: W.G. Aston

sources.description: An English translation of the Nihon Shoki, another important source of Japanese mythology.
